text: Hi and welcome to This is Markitecture. Today I'm joined by Ruggiero from Docebo.
Thank you Fabio for having me. It's a pleasure. So Ruggiero could you tell us a little bit more
about Docebo? Sure. So Docebo produces AI powered learning e-learning tools and within Docebo I
worked in the Shape team. Okay so this Shape team what do you do for Docebo? What's your project,
your goal? Yes so Shape aims to produce e-learning tools starting from complex documents like business
processes or scientific papers or marketing presentations and it aims to produce an e-learning
pills of about five minutes. Okay so what do you do with these documents? Yeah so our main goal is to
reduce the noise from the document and because we don't want really to provide information that are
not helpful in the e-learning journey of the final user and right now we are dealing with about 10
10 to 20 documents a day and this specific architecture is able to handle about 30 to 50 pages per minute.
Oh okay so these are the numbers so let's get into the the technological details how does this work?
Sure so first of all there are some files. Okay so these are the ones provided by the end users
and these are PDFs? Yeah mainly PDFs. Okay that they use images. Text and images so rich files that the user uploads and we store them into an S3 bucket.
Once the files are uploaded and stored into S3 we send events through the main bus with the reference to those files

and every event. And every event triggers a step function powered by lambda functions. And the first step we do is to convert each page into an image just to be able to analyze the image with a machine, a custom machine learning model that is deployed into a SageMaker real-time endpoint.
Once we analyze the data. Once we analyze the data. Once we analyze the data out of the page we do some text extraction using a text structure, a managed text structure API.
And once we analyze the data out of the page we do some post processing like figuring the reading order or better some more semantic analysis.
And then once the document is ready and we figure out whether there is noise and where the noise is we can send them back.
We can send events back through the main bus just to notify that the process is ready.
So in essence you have many lambdas you go through different phases and then whatever you prepare would be an input for other part of the table.
Exactly.
To work on the documents and have them clean in some kind of way.
Exactly.
Okay, good. Is there any metrics that you could share about how good you are treating the documents?
Yeah, sure. So as I was saying before, the main challenge is to be able to reduce the noise in the final output.
And in that sense we managed to reduce the noise by about 80% with this specific architecture.
So that's very good. So that means that you remove stuff and the other teams will not have to work on that stuff, which is not meaningful to your custom.
Okay, nice. So I got an overview. Is there any area in the architecture where you learn something or that you changed over time because it was not working the way you wanted?
Yeah, so the shape team had never deployed a custom machine learning model into production.
And in that regard SageMaker was really helpful, not only for the deploying phase, but also helpful in helping us to manage the scaling part of the infrastructure.
And a natural evolution of this architecture could be using SageMaker serverless endpoint instead of the real-time endpoint.
Okay, so you want that to be something like pay-per-use?
Yeah, sure. Just to cut on costs.
Yeah, because the whole infrastructure is serverless. This is the only endpoint that is sitting there 24 by 7.
Yeah.
So yeah, that really makes sense. No, it's very compelling architecture. Thank you very much for sharing that, Ruggiero.
Thank you for having me again.
And thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
How to get started, this is the business of Punxand.
How to get started.
